Case Study · Product Design · 2025

Smartizen
Academy

Designing a dual-sided platform that closes the visibility gap between early childhood educators and the parents who trust them.

EdTech · 0–5 yrs
iOS · Mobile
Parents + Facilitators
Smartizen
  • Problem
  • System
  • Decisions
  • Tradeoffs
  • Reflection
01 — The Problem

A trust gap disguised as
a communication gap

Early childhood programs run on a paradox. The most developmentally critical years of a child's life happen almost entirely out of a parent's sight. A facilitator spends 45 minutes with a toddler and holds rich, observable data — what the child struggled with, what sparked joy, what milestone just tipped. That data evaporates by pickup time.

Parents, meanwhile, fill the void with anxiety. They invent proxies — asking how lunch went, scrutinizing moods at the door. The real information never travels.

The Black Box Session

What happens inside a session is almost entirely invisible to parents. Verbal summaries at pickup are compressed, inconsistent, and quickly forgotten.

No Developmental Continuity

Growth doesn't happen only in class. But without a feedback loop, parents can't extend learning at home — they don't know what to reinforce or how.

Facilitator Overhead

Documenting sessions, writing reports, notifying parents, and managing schedules all happen in disconnected tools — or not at all — creating burnout and inconsistency.

No Shared Language

Parents and facilitators have no common vocabulary for child development. Cognitive, motor, language, memory — these distinctions are meaningful only if both sides share the same framework.

Core Insight
"The goal wasn't to build a reporting tool. It was to rebuild the relationship between two people who both care deeply about the same child — but rarely speak the same language."
02 — The System

One product.
Two jobs to be done.

Most EdTech products solve one side of the equation. Smartizen had to solve both simultaneously — and the two sides had almost opposite mental models, available time, and technical comfort levels.

System Architecture — How the two sides interlock

Parent App

Visibility Layer

  • Live session status
  • Daily Brain Boost summary
  • Growth scores by domain
  • Moments photo feed
  • Facilitator messaging
  • Home activity suggestions
Facilitator App

Operations Layer

  • Session management
  • Per-child scoring
  • Photo tagging by domain
  • Report writing & publishing
  • Broadcast announcements
  • Attendance tracking
Shared Data

The Bridge

  • Whole-brain framework
  • 4 developmental domains
  • Milestone taxonomy
  • Session → report pipeline
  • Photo attribution per child
  • Real-time activity status
03 — Key Design Decisions

The thinking behind
the choices

Decision 01

OTP-first auth, not passwords

Both parents and facilitators sign in via phone OTP rather than email-password. The two roles are visually distinguished — warm pink for parents, deep teal for facilitators — before a single digit is entered.

Why this matters

The target parent demographic skews toward adults who manage many passwords and often abandon accounts after a forgotten credential. OTP removes the friction at the only point where users can leave forever. The visual role-split also prevents facilitators from accidentally operating in the wrong context.

Decision 02

The parent home as a daily briefing

The parent home screen is structured like a morning briefing, not a dashboard. It leads with the child's live session status, then today's activities, then moments from the session, then a facilitator note — roughly in the order a parent would naturally want to know.

Why this matters

Dashboards optimise for exploration. Briefings optimise for the anxious parent checking their phone at 11am. By sequencing information around a parent's emotional state rather than information hierarchy, the home screen answers the real question: "Is my child okay and what did they do today?"

Decision 03

Four developmental domains as the spine

Every score, photo, activity, and report in Smartizen is tagged to one of four domains: Memory, Language, Motor, and Cognitive Speed. This taxonomy runs through both sides of the product — facilitators tag photos by domain, parents see growth by domain, games are categorised by domain.

Why this matters

Without a shared framework, data is just noise. The four-domain model creates a common language that lets a facilitator's session observation become a parent's growth insight become a home activity recommendation — all without any translation. It also allows the product to surface non-obvious things: "Your child's motor skills are strong but cognitive speed is lagging" is a meaningful, actionable observation that generic check-ins can't produce.

Decision 04

Session as the atomic unit of work

For facilitators, the session is the core object. Starting a live session, marking attendance, uploading and tagging photos, writing reports — all flow from a single session entry. Reports can be written after the fact, and the system surfaces which sessions still have pending reports.

Why this matters

Facilitators are time-poor. If documentation is disconnected from the session it describes, it doesn't get done. Tying all reporting work to a specific session creates a clear job: close the session, close the loop. The "pending reports" alert system applies the right social pressure — facilitators see which children are waiting for their update.

Decision 05

Connect as context, not just chat

The messaging module isn't generic chat. It surfaces facilitator context before messages — their role, last active time, a preview of their last message — and provides parents with quick-reply templates ("Is Emma progressing well?", "What can I do at home?") that lower the barrier to asking important questions.

Why this matters

Parents often don't know what to ask. Blank message fields are paralyzing when the stakes feel high. Quick replies serve a dual function: they reduce friction for parents and they signal to facilitators the kind of questions that matter. The announcements section also ensures that broadcast communication (events, breaks, material changes) doesn't get buried in 1:1 messages.

Decision 06

Needs Attention as a triage surface

The facilitator home doesn't list all children equally. It surfaces a "Needs Attention" list: children with pending scores, overdue reports, or recent parent requests. The most urgent items are visually prominent; completed ones recede.

Why this matters

A facilitator managing 20+ children across 4 programs cannot give equal attention to everything. Rather than displaying a flat list and trusting the facilitator to triage mentally, the system does the prioritisation. This is a meaningful shift from a tool that stores information to a tool that directs action — the difference between a filing cabinet and a co-pilot.

04 — Tradeoffs Made

Every choice is also
a rejection

Rejected

Single app for all users

One app that detects your role and adapts the interface. Simpler to distribute, one codebase to maintain.

Chosen

Role-split from the first screen

Parent and facilitator enter through distinct visual identities from the landing screen. The mental model is never shared. This preserves context and prevents facilitators from accidentally sending a parent's message to the wrong thread.

Rejected

Free-form progress notes

Let facilitators write whatever they observe in plain text. Maximum flexibility, minimum structure.

Chosen

Structured domain scoring + notes

Scores per domain, then a note field. This sacrifices freedom for comparability — the same child can be tracked across weeks, and cross-child patterns can emerge within a program.

Rejected

Real-time livestream of sessions

Let parents watch sessions via camera feed for maximum transparency.

Chosen

Curated asynchronous moments

Facilitators capture and tag meaningful photos during sessions, which appear in the parent feed after review. This creates a narrative rather than surveillance, and respects all children's privacy — not just your own child's.

Rejected

AI-generated progress reports

Automatically summarise session scores into a report that the facilitator approves with one tap.

Chosen

Facilitator-authored reports with structured scaffolding

Reports are written by facilitators, but the interface structures what's needed and creates accountability (pending reports surface clearly). Human authorship preserves the relationship — parents know there's a real person watching their child.

05 — What I'd Push Further

Open questions
worth asking

The 10-step onboarding

Child onboarding runs 10 steps including smart recognition photos, medical info, and consent. This is thorough — but it's also the moment of highest drop-off risk. I'd want to test whether steps 5–9 could be deferred to after first value is delivered.

The home activity gap

Home activity suggestions ("Try this at home: Color Sorting Game") are a strong idea — but the current design doesn't close the loop. Did the parent try it? Did the child engage? That feedback would make the system substantially smarter over time.

Growth scores as motivation

Domain scores expressed as percentages are clinically useful but emotionally cold. For parents of toddlers, "74% Memory" may not feel as meaningful as "Emma can now recall sequences of 4 objects." The milestone layer exists — the question is whether it leads or follows the numbers.
